behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them
behaviors.justification: The behavior explicitly requires testing across multiple microservice sources with different log formats. A Unit test with abstracted data would miss bugs in trace ID extraction from heterogeneous formats (e.g., different timestamp formats, field names, or nesting structures across services). Integration-level testing with multiple mock log files or realistic data structures from each microservice type is needed to catch format-specific parsing failures that would occur in production.
behaviors.test_description: Create test log files simulating multiple microservices (API, database, cache) where logs share trace IDs but arrive in different orders and use service-specific field names. Load and correlate the logs, then verify all entries with matching trace IDs are grouped together. Test edge cases: trace IDs appearing in only some services, multiple trace IDs per service, and trace ID format variations across services.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services
behaviors.justification: This is pure data-processing logic. Clock skew handling is an ordering algorithm that can be tested with synthetic, in-process log entries. A Unit test can create entries with deliberate skew (e.g., service A +200ms, service B −300ms) and verify ordering correctness without requiring real I/O, file system, or subprocess interaction. The timestamp extraction itself is a separate concern; the ordering logic is stateless.
behaviors.test_description: Create in-memory test log entries with timestamps from different services, deliberately introducing clock skew (e.g., one service 200ms ahead, another 300ms behind). Build a timeline view and verify entries are ordered by true timestamp despite the skew. Test boundary conditions: entries with identical timestamps, entries exactly at the 500ms tolerance threshold, and gradual vs. abrupt skew transitions.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report
behaviors.justification: While the p99 calculation and spike-detection logic are stateless functions, the behavior requires latencies to be correctly extracted from correlated log entries. A Unit test isolating the p99 algorithm would miss failures in trace correlation (e.g., matching logs by trace ID incorrectly, causing latency miscalculation). Integration testing with end-to-end log correlation, latency calculation, and spike detection catches real failures where the spike detection logic is correct but integration with log correlation is broken.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple complete request traces (log sequences from multiple services sharing a trace ID) with baseline latencies (p99 ≈ 100ms). Inject a burst of slow traces (p99 > 300ms). Verify the anomaly report correctly identifies and flags the spike. Test edge cases: baseline rising gradually vs. sudden spike, spikes in different time windows, correct p99 percentile calculation, and whether slow traces are still properly grouped and measured before spike detection.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported
behaviors.justification: A Unit test of JSON parsing error handling in isolation (e.g., testing that `json.loads()` raises on bad input) does not verify that the pipeline gracefully continues with valid entries interspersed. Integration testing is needed to ensure that when malformed entries are encountered in a live stream, the parser skips them, the pipeline doesn't halt, and the count is correctly accumulated. Real failure modes (partial recovery, off-by-one in counting, state corruption after error) are only visible when testing the full pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Create a log input stream mixing valid JSON entries with malformed ones (invalid syntax, truncated JSON, missing required fields, wrong data types). Run the full pipeline and verify parsing completes without crash, all valid entries are processed, and the skipped count matches malformed entries exactly. Test variations: clusters of consecutive malformed entries, partially valid entries, different error types (unexpected characters, encoding issues), and verify the pipeline resumes correctly after each skip.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind
behaviors.justification: Throughput and concurrency under realistic load cannot be tested at Integration level. The behavior requires real I/O patterns, actual timing, and OS-level resource constraints. Failures—dropped entries, buffer overflow, thread starvation, memory exhaustion, deadlock under contention—only appear when the system is stressed with real concurrent streams at realistic rates. Mocking I/O or using synthetic in-memory loads would hide kernel buffer limits, file descriptor exhaustion, and scheduling issues that manifest under real load.
behaviors.test_description: Generate a load test feeding 10,000 structured log entries per second into the tool from multiple concurrent source streams (simulating multiple microservices). Measure that all entries are processed (none dropped or lost), the burst completes without degradation, and resource usage remains stable. Monitor for failure modes: memory leaks, thread explosion, input buffer overflow, delayed processing that cascades. Repeat the burst multiple times to ensure the tool doesn't eventually fall behind or accumulate state degradation.
